title: Introducing this site
summary: A quick note on what this site is for, and why it was built in a fast, deliberately simple first pass.

content.text: This site is a simple place to collect selected projects, product work, and short notes about how they are being built. Rather than scatter that context across different profiles and repos, it felt better to put everything in one clear home.

The first version came together quickly because the goal was not to design a huge platform. It was to get something useful online: a lightweight homepage, a small projects section, and a blog that can publish static posts without adding a CMS or extra infrastructure.

items: The site uses a straightforward Next.js setup with static generation.
Projects and writing are presented with just enough structure to be easy to update.
The blog content lives in JSON, which keeps publishing fast and the build predictable.

content.text: That quick development pace was part of the point. A personal site should be easy to maintain, easy to extend, and useful from day one. This version does that, and it leaves plenty of room to refine the details over time.

title: Designing a static AI-assisted blog
summary: A simple pattern for publishing weekly posts from JSON while keeping validation strict and deployment predictable.

content.text: This site stays deliberately simple: blog posts live in a JSON file, the build validates them, and GitHub Pages serves the exported result.

items: The content format is explicit, so an AI writer has a clear contract to follow.
The build fails early if a post has the wrong shape or an invalid slug.
Deployment stays static, with no CMS or runtime database to manage.

content.text: That combination keeps the authoring flow lightweight without making the site fragile.

title: From prototype to something usable
summary: This week was about turning rough product ideas into something people can actually use, while also launching a simple site to show the work in public.

content.text: A lot of this week went into making things feel more real. Rather than chasing flashy new surfaces, the work focused on the pieces that make a product feel usable: clearer flows, better structure, fewer rough edges, and a simpler way to talk about the work in public.
Making Draft Path feel like a real product

The biggest chunk of coding time went into Draft Path. One thread was about the front door: putting proper sign-in and sign-up in place, supporting both password-based login and social sign-in, and tightening the way protected parts of the app behave. It is not glamorous work, but it is the kind of thing that turns a demo into something a person could actually come back to and use.

Another thread was about helping people capture ideas with a bit more shape. The new idea flow now pauses to ask for one clarifying step before saving, which is a small interaction on the surface but an important product decision underneath. It shifts the experience away from dumping text into a box and toward helping a rough thought become something more useful.

items: Finished the first proper authentication setup and the surrounding sign-in, sign-up, and sign-out flow.
Merged the guided idea-entry work so concept capture feels more structured and intentional.
Spent time on the quality layer too: reusable components, error handling, accessibility fixes, dependency updates, and test clean-up.

content.text: Launching a public home for the work

Alongside the product work, this week also included building and launching this site. It started as a very small personal homepage, then quickly picked up a projects section, a blog, the first posts, and the GitHub Pages setup needed to make it all public. That matters because it creates a clearer place to show what is being built instead of leaving the work scattered across repos and notes.

What ties the week together is a simple theme: moving from placeholders to working paths. Sign-in now works. The idea flow has more shape. The site is live. A lot of the effort went into foundations and polish rather than headline features, but that is exactly what makes the next stage of building easier and more credible.
